page_content:
# Passage: 3  
The well-established contribution of psychological factors to sleep disturbances has led to the increased use of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), which contains elements of both Behavioral Therapy (BT) and Cognitive Therapy (CT), to treat insomnia. In order to help patients learn to associate the bedroom with sleep and being sleepy, BT involves limiting bedroom use to sleeping. By changing patients’ beliefs about sleep and sleeplessness, CT draws on theories of attitude and behavioral change by addressing unhelpful beliefs and misperceptions about sleep and sleeplessness. One goal of the CT might be to help the patient break the cycle of worrying about sleeplessness, which leads to more sleeplessness and hence more worry.  
Researchers were interested in the comparative effectiveness of BT, CT, and CBT in treating insomnia and in extending the studied treatment outcomes to include the improvement of poor daytime functioning linked to insomnia. They recruited 188 adults who met multiple criteria for insomnia. Fewer than 12% of interested individuals met the rigorous exclusion criteria, including irregular sleep schedules and unwillingness to reduce alcohol and caffeine ingestion. Participants were randomly assigned to one of the three therapies (BT, CT, and CBT), all of which involved eight weekly individual sessions with a trained therapist. Participants completed measures of insomnia severity and work/social adjustment (daytime functioning across work, home, social, and interpersonal activities) at three time points: before the start of therapy (baseline), immediately following therapy, and at a 6-month follow-up.  
There were no baseline differences among the three groups of participants on demographic variables, medical comorbidity, or psychological comorbidity. Session attendance was high (94.2%) and comparable across therapies. Overall attrition rates were low and were similar across the three therapy groups. The findings for insomnia severity are shown in Figure 1.  
FB-16-1-204FB_80453-00P1.gif  
Figure 1Group means of self-reported insomnia severity by therapy condition at baseline, immediately following therapy, and after 6 months (Note: * indicates p < .05.)
Work/social adjustment improved from baseline to after therapy among all therapy groups (although less so for BT), and this improvement was sustained across therapies at the 6-month follow-up.
